title: Streaming progress for long-running operations remains usable
description:
# Story: Streaming progress for long-running operations remains usable

## Intent

As an MCP client (Cursor or similar) consuming `execute_tactus`, I want to see incremental progress for operations that take more than a few seconds, so that I can show users meaningful feedback and can confirm the agent's orchestration is doing useful work.

## Gherkin

```gherkin
Feature: Long-running operations stream progress to the MCP client

  Scenario: Synthetic evaluation with streamed progress
    Given a stubbed plexus.evaluation.run that simulates 1000 items processed over 60 seconds
    And a boot prompt that teaches the agent to invoke evaluations
    And an MCP client that supports streaming notifications
    When a frontier LLM invokes execute_tactus with code that calls plexus.evaluation.run
    Then the MCP client receives at least one streaming progress event per 5 seconds during the call
    And each event reports current progress (e.g., items_processed, current_metric, cost_so_far)
    And the final response includes the completed result with full cost accounting

  Scenario: Synthetic budget exhaustion mid-flight
    Given the same stubbed evaluation
    And a per-call budget that is intentionally too small to complete the full operation
    When the LLM invokes the operation
    Then the operation halts gracefully when budget is exhausted
    And the final response indicates partial: true with the work completed so far
    And cost reporting accurately reflects the spend before halting
```

## Definition of Done

- Stub harness produces realistic streaming events for the simulated evaluation.
- Streaming events visible to the MCP client during the call (verified end-to-end against at least one real client, ideally Cursor).
- Partial-results behavior on budget exhaustion verified.
- Findings recorded: does the streaming UX feel usable to a human watching, or do we need design work in Epic 7?
